title: Predicting massive helium-3 release from metal tritides using simple mechanical modeling
authors: Berengere Evin, Dorian Gaboriau, Mathieu Segard, Sylvain Challet, Arnaud Fabre, Stephanie Thiebaut
abstract: This letter is presenting a simple but effective mechanism that explains why ,during tritium aging, metal tritides retain most helium-3 for years and then suddenly release massive amount. The mechanism is based on the hypothesis that dislocations blocking could explain the sudden change of behavior. The modeling of this phenomenon combine a mechanical and microstructural approach. The calculations made with this mechanism fit all the aging data acquired on aged palladium tritide
summary: This paper addresses the mechanism behind the sudden release of helium-3 from metal tritides like palladium tritide. The authors propose that as helium bubbles grow, they act as precipitates that impede dislocation motion, eventually reaching a hardness threshold that triggers massive gas release. By integrating dislocation physics into a continuum mechanics model, they accurately predict the timing and scale of this desorption. This mechanistic approach replaces older empirical models with a more predictive framework based on fundamental material properties.
main_problem: Explaining the sudden transition from long-term helium-3 retention to massive desorption in metal tritides during tritium aging.
main_result: A new dislocation-based mechanical model successfully predicts the helium desorption threshold and bubble growth evolution, matching experimental data more accurately than previous semi-empirical models.
method: The study combines continuum mechanics for bubble growth with a dislocation-based mechanical model, comparing the results against experimental TEM, STEM, EELS, and NMR data.
model_or_system: The system is palladium tritide (PdTx) where helium-3 produced by tritium decay is trapped in nanobubbles that act as solid-state precipitates.
key_observables: Bubble radius, bubble pressure, helium-to-palladium ratio (He/Pd), and dislocation mobility/strains.
important_parameters: Shear modulus (47 GPa), Burgers vector (0.27 nm), yield strength (170 MPa), and critical precipitate radius (~1.4 nm).
main_assumptions: The desorption threshold is reached when dislocation mobility is blocked due to reaching maximum material hardness (equality of Orowan and Friedel strains).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows TEM images of palladium powder; Figure 2 shows bubble radius evolution; Figure 3 shows bubble pressure evolution; Figure 4 shows the He/Pd ratio vs. theoretical ratio.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of helium release, presents a new dislocation-based modeling approach, compares this model to previous semi-empirical methods, validates the model against experimental measurements, and discusses limitations regarding pressure estimation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1 – High Angle Annular Dark Field images of palladium powder aged 6.5 years (He/Pdr=0.23) (left) and over 20 years (He/Pdth=0.46) (right) under tritium.

Figure 2 – Bubble radius evolution over time. New dislocations modeling (solid line) is compared to previous semi-empirical modeling (dotted line) and experimental measurements of TEM and STEM tomography [18] (cross).

Figure 3 – Bubble pressure as a function of time. New dislocations modeling (solid line) is compared to previous semi-empirical modeling (dotted line) and experimental measurements of EELS [8] and NMR (cross).
Figure 4 presents the evolution of He/Pdr ratio over the He/Pdth ratio.

Figure 4 – He/Pd ratio evolution as a function of the theoretical He/Pd (no helium release). New dislocations modeling (solid line) is compared to semi-empirical modeling (dotted line) and experimental measurements of gas analysis (cross) [18].
